title: Union Carbide Corporation v. Union of India
relevant_acts: Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of Claims) Act 1985
summary: Settlement of ₹715 crore (USD 470 million) for victims of the 1984 Bhopal gas tragedy that killed thousands. The Court approved the settlement between UCC and Government of India acting as parens patriae for victims. It also made doctrine of absolute liability applicable — industrial enterprises engaged in hazardous activities are absolutely liable for harm caused.
key_holdings: Doctrine of absolute liability applies to enterprises engaged in hazardous activities
Government can act as parens patriae to represent disaster victims
Courts have jurisdiction to approve class-action type settlements
Amount of compensation must be 'just, equitable and reasonable'
key_quotes: Enterprises which are engaged in hazardous activity owe an absolute and non-delegable duty to the community.
There are no exceptions to the principle of absolute liability.
why_important: Established absolute liability doctrine (stronger than strict liability from Rylands v Fletcher). Set precedent for corporate liability in industrial disasters. Led to Environment Protection Act and related legislation.
relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Absolute liability doctrine directly impacts corporate insurance requirements and financial risk provisioning.
